race.name: Helsinki Gran Fondo
race.tagline: Scenic paved routes from city to countryside, non-competitive gran fondo for all levels

climate.description: No specific data available; typical late August in Helsinki features mild summer weather.

features: Paved roads combining urban and rural landscapes with quality surfaces and moderate challenge

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 64. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 61.

course_description.character: Diverse paved journey from vibrant city to peaceful countryside, starting with controlled lead-out to Leppävaara.

logistics.transport: Start/finish at Helsinki Velodrome; arrive 60min early for bib pickup with photo ID; two waves 1hr apart (160km first).

history.origin_story: Recurring event returning in 2026; gran fondo concept from 1970s Italy emphasizing non-competitive long rides.

biased_opinion.summary: Helsinki Gran Fondo offers an accessible, scenic paved ride ideal for enthusiasts seeking community and enjoyment over competition, though it lacks gravel challenge and detailed route data until spring 2026.

final_verdict.one_liner: Welcoming non-race gran fondo with great value and Finnish scenery.
final_verdict.should_you_race: A solid choice for road cyclists wanting a supported, paced group ride through urban-rural contrasts, with e-bikes welcome and strong community elements, but awaits route details for full assessment.

citations.label: Register for the 2026 Helsinki Gran Fondo
Route Maps and Ride Details - Helsinki Gran Fondo

youtube_data.search_query: Helsinki Gran Fondo gran fondo cycling Helsinki Velodrome

videos.title: Gran Fondo il Lombardia - The Fondo of the Falling Leaves | Cycling Lake Como
videos.description:
My final big ride of the year - this time in Italy, in the Lake Como region for Gran Fondo Il Lombardia, with Andiamo Cycling and 2000 other cyclists !  x2 Iconic climbs, including Colma di Sormano with the final 2km known as Muro di Sormano - the WALL of Sormano - a worthy title given the average gradient of 16.5%. After which we have a beautiful descent with stunning views to Lake Como, and the peleton hits full gas to transition 15km to the town of Bellagio to take on the 2nd very iconic ascent of Madonna Del Ghisallo, with the Church in honour of cyclists at the peak!     

As always a beautiful experience in Italy with the impeccable Andiamo Cycling (I am not sponsored), we hope you enjoy  ! 

To translate the subtitles to your preferred language, please go to the settings cog on the video, and make sure subtitles are turned on.  Then select auto-translate, and then select the language you prefer.

Please leave those comments and hit that subscribe 

Thanks ever so much for all of your support! And whoever you are, what ever your do, please remember to LIVE THRIVE AND STAY HEALTHY ! 

Follow my journey  
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/bikeracingwithoutmercy/
Strava: https://www.strava.com/athletes/BRWM
tags: the race of the falling leaves

videos.title: Rose X-Lite CRS 3100 Di2 - Bike of the Year - Best Race
videos.description: The new race chassis from Rose is a complete departure from the old X-LIte. Out go the massively oversized round tubes and in comes a selection of squared-off profiles developed on from the CGF endurance bike, which debuted in 2014.
videos.transcript:
[Music]
[Applause]
So here we have the rose. Xlite CRS 3100
for 2015. This is Cycling Plus's best
race bike.
For those of you not familiar with Rose,
Rose is a massive German online brand.
They offer all their own bikes
throughout Mountain Biking Road, BMX,
etc., etc. Everything they make is fully
developed and tested by their in-house
team, and it's also raced by the teams
that they sponsor. So, the CRS is a bike
that's been developed on from the CGF,
which debuted last year. Now the CGF is
the carbon Grand Fondo and this one is
the carbon rad sport. So this is their
race bike. It's a extremely extremely
welldesigned and developed frame. The
frame set uh in this guys weighs just
910 g. Now they do offer like a prolevel
version of it which the team guys ride
and that weight drops down to 800 which
is pretty impressive. But both share
this all new Xlite fork which weighs
just 315 g. It's one of the lightest
forks on the market right now. So, this
fork has also been tested independently
at a German university and found to have
the the best stiffness to weight ratio
of any fork on the market right now. I
guess what we're saying is that that the
heart of this bike has got some really
really seriously good tech going on.
What a lot of you will find the most
surprising about this bike is the price.
Currently, it's sitting around £2,100,
but Ros's prices do fluctuate as
everything they sell is priced in euros,
so it relies on the daily exchange rate
really. So componentry wise we've got
Richie WCS carbon for the bar stem and
seat post. It's a monolink seat post. So
it's topped with cellular Italia's
monolink SLS. Now the monolink concept
was designed to allow Calia to design a
saddle system they call friction free
that allows the nose of the saddle to
remain very very narrow much much
further back. That design is there so
that uh as you're pedaling you're not
getting any rub on the side of the
saddle you know sort of chaf free. They
claim performance benefits, but we like
the system. But for us, it's just more
of a comfort thing. So, I guess the the
thing that is going to create the
headlines on this bike, especially at
its price, is the fact that uh it's the
only bike we've got in the test this
year that's running Di2. So, it's the
new Altegra Di2 11speed. It's really,
really nicely integrated within this
frame. Everything's fully internally
rooted. You've got the internal seat
post. Now, of course, there's no removal
of the battery to be able to charge it,
etc. You simply plug your charger up
into the control box on the stem and
charge it very intimately whenever it
needs it. So, of course, with Di2, be it
Ortega or Jurass, um you get that super
super sharp, crisp shifting all the
time, completely consistent. It doesn't
matter if you're running a bad chain
line, it's sorted out for you. So, you
don't get any chain rub. It's a nice
smooth quiet system. We've been using
the i2 now for a couple of years, and
we're still finding that charging cycles
are probably once every 3 months or so.
any fears you might have that you might
be out riding and you're you know you
run out of juice simply doesn't happen.
And then the final piece of the puzzle
on the equipment side of things is the
DT Swiss die cut wheels. Now this is
quite a high level wheel for DT and
you've got that diecut hub design which
enables supposed to be straight pull.
They're aerobladed. It's a nice light
wheel set and uh it's no surprise that
once you add that into the rest of this
super light chassis and top- end
equipment, you end up with a bike that
weighs just 6.9 kilos, which for a bike
that's uh circling around the just over
2,000 lb mark. That's remarkably light.
And so this CRS, the way it rides, it's
super super aggressive. It's very kind
of uh it's very almost old school
German, you know, so it's got super
steep angles, you know, parallel 73.5°
on that and a wheelbase that's just 996
mil, which is super super tight. You
know, this bike literally you can just
flick it around anywhere. If you want to
get involved in the hustle and bustle of
of kind of crit racing, then this would
be a really nice option to do so. And so
with the factors we've got on this bike,
great equipment, fantastic drivetrain,
brilliant wheels, super light chassis,
massively aggressive geometry with that
short wheelbase and the true agility of
it, it's easy to see why Cycling Plus
has made this race bike of the year for
2015.

videos.title: Miltary Mad Science | Interactive 2011 | SXSW
videos.description: For more than 50 years the mad scientists at the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency—aka DARPA, the outrageous research arm of the Pentagon—have been launching the most disruptive technologies on earth, living up to their mantra of "high risk—high payoff." We have DARPA to thank for the personal computer, the Internet, the Berkeley Unix system, most of NASA, and countless crazy military innovations. Their mission is to think beyond the possible and forever be three decades ahead. In this talk we will dig into, and present the relevant parts of, DARPA's $3 billion-dollar budget, pulling out the most amazing and most-likely-to-reach-fruition projects. Think electromagnetic bazookas, telepathic soldiers, ape-inspired robots, memory chips in brains, shapeshifting planes and boats. It might sound like sci-fi, but given its inspired history it seems that analyzing DARPA's current projects will give us one of the clearest views into our future reality. Fasten your seat belts.

key_challenges.name: Miro de Samano (Wall of Samano)
key_challenges.description: A brutal 2 km section at 16.5% gradient on a narrow path, described as 'I want my mummy gradients' that creates cycling theater. Most riders elect to take this route despite an easier right turn option that adds 2 km.

key_challenges.name: Conano Climb
key_challenges.description: A 5.5 km section at 6.5% gradient that leads directly into the Wall of Samano. The full climb takes about an hour and 13 minutes for experienced riders, longer with traffic.

race_day_tips.text: For the first 10 km, stay right, hold your line, and yield where needed due to chaotic Italian Grand Fondo start conditions.
Don't try to leave breaking room in the bunch because other riders will simply take the space, causing you to slip back into more dangerous positions.
Take the major climbs easy at the beginning to avoid going too hard too early.

additional_quotes.text: It's exhilarating to ride in a bunch in a crit race with structure and a known circuit, but terrifying in a fondo with so so many cyclists and all of the road furniture of a city.

rider_intel.search_text: This appears to be coverage of Grand Fondo Il Lombardier rather than Helsinki Gran Fondo, based on the transcript content. The race features extremely chaotic Italian Grand Fondo start conditions with dangerous bunch riding through city streets. The major challenge is a two-part climbing sequence: first the Conano climb at 5.5 km and 6.5% gradient, followed immediately by the Wall of Samano (Miro de Samano) - a brutal 2 km section at 16.5% gradient on narrow paths. Riders can avoid the wall by turning right and adding 2 km distance with easier gradients, but most elect to take on the steep challenge. The full climb sequence takes experienced riders about an hour. Early crashes occur even on straight, downhill sections. The first 10 km require extreme caution due to chaotic conditions with hundreds of cyclists navigating city road furniture.
